name: playwright
context: Write and run cross-browser end-to-end tests with Playwright — auto-waiting, network mocking, visual regression, codegen, trace viewer, parallel execution
info: Playwright is Microsoft's cross-browser end-to-end testing framework for web applications. It drives Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit with a single API, providing auto-waiting that eliminates flaky sleeps, network interception for mocking API responses, visual comparison via screenshots and snapshots, codegen for recording user actions as test code, and a trace viewer for debugging failures. Tests run in parallel across configurable projects (browser/device combos) with isolated browser contexts. The @playwright/test package includes a built-in test runner with fixtures (page, context, browser), expect assertions, and HTML reporter. Use [[testing/playwright]] when testing web UI flows, browser automation, form interactions, authentication flows, or visual regression. Pair with [[testing/ci]] for headless CI execution and [[testing/mocking]] for broader mocking strategies.
triggers: mock api responses in e2e tests
debug failing e2e tests with trace

sections.title: Quick start — install, init, first test
sections.body: Run `npm init playwright@latest` to scaffold a project with config, example tests, and browser installs. Or add to existing: `npm install -D @playwright/test && npx playwright install --with-deps`. Write tests in `tests/*.spec.ts` using `test()` and `expect()` from `@playwright/test`. Run with `npx playwright test`. Use `--ui` for interactive mode, `--headed` to see the browser, `--project=chromium` for a single browser. The init wizard lets you choose TypeScript/JavaScript, test directory, and GitHub Actions setup.

sections.title: Finding elements — getByRole, getByText, getByTestId
sections.body: Playwright recommends user-facing locators that survive refactors. `getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' })` is most resilient — it uses ARIA roles and accessible names. `getByLabel('Email')` finds form fields by label association. `getByText('Welcome')` matches visible text. `getByPlaceholder('Search...')` targets inputs. `getByTestId('nav')` uses data-testid attributes as an explicit contract. Fall back to `locator('css')` or `locator('xpath')` only when semantic locators are impossible. Chain with `.filter({ hasText: 'foo' })` or `.nth(0)`. Use `.first()` and `.last()` for ordered lists. All locators auto-wait for the element to be actionable before interacting.

sections.title: Assertions — expect(locator) auto-retrying checks
sections.body: Playwright assertions auto-wait and auto-retry. `expect(locator).toBeVisible()` waits for the element to appear. `toHaveText('exact')` and `toContainText('partial')` check text. `toHaveCount(n)` verifies element count. `toBeEnabled()`, `toBeDisabled()`, `toBeChecked()` check form state. `toHaveValue('v')` checks input value. `toHaveAttribute('href', '/home')` checks attributes. `toHaveClass(/active/)` checks CSS classes. Page-level: `expect(page).toHaveTitle()`, `expect(page).toHaveURL()`. Use `expect.soft()` for non-blocking assertions that report at the end. Negate with `.not`: `expect(locator).not.toBeVisible()`. Default timeout is 5000ms, configurable via `expect: { timeout: 10000 }` in config.

sections.title: Intercepting and mocking API calls
sections.body: Use `page.route(urlPattern, handler)` to intercept network requests. `route.fulfill({ status, body, contentType })` returns a mock response without hitting the server. `route.fetch()` sends the real request and lets you modify the response before fulfilling. `route.abort()` blocks the request entirely — useful for disabling analytics or ads in tests. URL patterns support glob (`**/api/products`) and regex. Use `page.waitForResponse(url)` to wait for a specific response after an action. Mock error states (500, 404, timeouts) to test error handling. Combine route handlers with `route.continue()` to pass through requests you do not want to mock. Routes are per-context — set them in a fixture to apply across tests.

sections.title: Screenshot and snapshot comparisons
sections.body: Use `expect(page).toHaveScreenshot('name.png')` for pixel-by-pixel comparison against a reference image. First run generates the reference in a `*-snapshots/` directory alongside your test file. Subsequent runs compare and fail on pixel differences. Use `maxDiffPixelRatio` or `maxDiffPixels` to allow tolerance for antialiasing and rendering differences. `mask: [locator]` hides dynamic content (timestamps, avatars) during comparison. `fullPage: true` captures the entire scrollable page. For text-based comparison, `expect(value).toMatchSnapshot('name.txt')` compares strings. Update all snapshots with `npx playwright test --update-snapshots`. Store snapshot files in version control so they serve as the visual contract.

sections.title: Reusing login state across tests
sections.body: Create an `auth.setup.ts` file that logs in via the UI and saves the browser's storage state (cookies, localStorage) with `page.context().storageState({ path: 'playwright/.auth/user.json' })`. In `playwright.config.ts`, define a `setup` project that runs `auth.setup.ts` and a main project that depends on it and uses `storageState: 'playwright/.auth/user.json'`. All tests in the dependent project start already authenticated — no repeated login flows. For multiple roles (admin, user), create separate setup files and separate projects with different storage state paths. Add `playwright/.auth/` to `.gitignore` since it contains session tokens.

sections.title: Recording tests with codegen
sections.body: Run `npx playwright codegen <url>` to open a browser and inspector. Every click, text input, and navigation is recorded as Playwright test code in real time. The inspector window shows generated code that you can copy into your test files. Use `--device='iPhone 13'` to emulate a mobile device during recording. Use `--output=tests/recorded.spec.ts` to save directly to a file. Codegen generates `getByRole` and `getByLabel` locators by default, producing resilient selectors. Use codegen as a starting point — refine the generated code by adding assertions, extracting page objects, and removing redundant waits.

sections.title: Debugging failed tests with traces
sections.body: Set `trace: 'on-first-retry'` in config to capture traces when tests fail and are retried. Traces record a timeline of every action, DOM snapshots before and after each step, network requests with headers and bodies, console logs, and the source code of the test. View traces with `npx playwright show-trace <path-to-trace.zip>` or open https://trace.playwright.dev and drag-drop the trace file. The trace viewer is the primary debugging tool — it shows exactly what happened at each step, making it far more effective than console.log debugging. For critical tests, use `trace: 'on'` to always capture traces regardless of pass/fail.

scripts.name: Run Playwright tests
scripts.description: Check environment, install browsers if needed, and run Playwright tests with configurable flags
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Check for @playwright/test
if ! node -e "require('@playwright/test')" 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "Error: @playwright/test is not installed."
  echo "Run: npm install -D @playwright/test"
  exit 1
fi

# Install browsers if missing
if ! npx playwright install --check 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "Installing Playwright browsers..."
  npx playwright install --with-deps
fi

# Forward all arguments to playwright test
exec npx playwright test "$@"

testFile.description: Specific test file or directory to run

project.description: Browser project to run (chromium, firefox, webkit)

grep.description: Only run tests matching this regex pattern

retries.description: Number of retries for failed tests

depends.reason: Playwright tests and config are written in TypeScript
